pattern: Sort every row independently, then the k-th removal step always deletes the k-th largest entry from each row — so the running maximum across rows at column position k (counted from the back after sorting) is exactly the amount added at that step.
hints: The order in which you delete duplicates of the same value inside a row never matters, so sort every row ascending first — that turns each row into a sequence you'll consume from the back.

After sorting all rows, the operation performed j times from the end always removes the j-th largest element of every row simultaneously, so line the rows up and look at them column by column from the right.
For each column position (from the largest down to the smallest), take the maximum value across all rows at that position and add it to a running total — that sum is the final answer.

constraints.note: m rows, n cols, 1<=m,n<=50, each entry in [1,100]

edge_inputs.description: single cell — the only value is removed in one step
single row, multiple columns — every value contributes since there's only one row to max over
single column, multiple rows — each step removes the single max across the whole column
two rows with ties within a row — either tied cell can be picked, result is unaffected
uniform grid — every step removes the same value from every row
every entry at the maximum allowed value 100
a row whose largest values are all small compared to another row's smallest — the running max per step is dominated by different rows at different steps, not always the same row
unsorted input rows — a solution that forgets to sort each row before consuming from the back would delete the wrong element first

stress.description: grid up to 50x50 with random values in [1,100]
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = max(1, size)
    n = max(1, min(50, size))
    grid = [[rng.randint(1, 100) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m if m <= 50 else 50)]
    return [grid]
